key_departures.impact: Franchise icon and all-time leading Bucs receiver departed after 12 seasons. Signed a 3yr/$42.4M deal ($16.3M guaranteed) with SF, worth up to $60.4M with incentives. Tampa had matching cap room but Evans wanted a new challenge. Leaves a massive void as the team's X-receiver, red zone weapon, and mentor. First time in his career he's left; injury-plagued 2025 (8 games, broken collarbone) was his first season without 1,000 receiving yards. Bucs have Emeka Egbuka, Chris Godwin, and Jalen McMillan on the roster but no true WR1 replacement.

Starting CB1 departing after 7 seasons. Signed 3yr/$36.75M ($12M guaranteed) with Pittsburgh. Had 14 starts in 2025, career-high 3 INTs, and PFF's 3rd-highest CB grade in 2025 with a 46.9 passer rating allowed (best in NFL). Significant loss — a genuinely elite outside corner leaving for market value the Bucs apparently couldn't or wouldn't match. Zyon McCollum (restructured) is a candidate to fill the void but has not proven starter-caliber reliability.

2022 second-round pick signed a 2yr/$13.75M deal ($9M guaranteed) with Houston. Had started 16 games in 2025 and totaled a career-high 39 tackles. Versatile 3-tech/5-tech with upside. His departure escalates the IDL need alongside Vita Vea, with A'Shawn Robinson signed as the immediate replacement on a one-year bridge deal.

Signed a 1yr/$2M deal with Washington. Was the Bucs' change-of-pace/early-down back after being supplanted as starter by Bucky Irving. 4,100+ scrimmage yards in four Bucs seasons. Departure was anticipated once Kenneth Gainwell was signed. Moderate loss — Gainwell is essentially a like-for-like replacement, possibly with more pass-catching upside.

One-year $14M deal in 2025 did not pan out — only 2.5 sacks in 13 games due to leg injuries. Did not re-sign and is still unsigned as of this report. Departure is net neutral to slightly positive given performance; team already pivoted to Al-Quadin Muhammad at EDGE. Still, EDGE depth remains a significant need after back-to-back disappointments at the position.

Signed a 1yr/$2M deal with Detroit. Was valued for versatility — could play safety, nickel, and cornerback. Described as 'invaluable' by beat reporters. 165 total tackles over three Bucs seasons. Departure creates a depth/versatility hole in the secondary and on special teams, partially offset by Miles Killebrew signing.

14-season Bucs legend still undecided between return to Tampa or retirement as of this report. Led defense with 114 tackles in 2025. ESPN reports decision is imminent — either re-signs with Bucs or retires; leaving for another team is not on the table. If he retires, the LB need escalates significantly despite Anzalone signing. Currently treated as uncertain pending; not flagged as a confirmed departure.

signings.analysis: Three-year re-signing locks in the Bucs' starting TE through 2028. Otton has back-to-back 59-catch seasons and is the most complete TE Tampa Bay has had in years as a pass-catcher, blocker, and chipper. At $10M AAV, this is mid-market TE money — fair value. The multi-year commitment signals the Bucs view him as their guy. TE goes from a need to a resolved position. Guarantees not yet disclosed but the 3-year structure signals real investment.
signings.notes: Guarantees TBD per reports at time of signing

signings.analysis: Two-year deal at $8.5M AAV is starter-tier money for a linebacker. Anzalone started all 16 games he played in 2025 for Detroit, logged 95 tackles (second-most on the team), and brings elite coverage skills against RBs and TEs. Has PFF 65.0+ grade in each of the last three seasons. Was a 5-time Lions team captain. At 31, he's not a long-term solution, and with Lavonte David's retirement decision still pending, the LB room could still need a draft pick. Two-year deal with meaningful money partially addresses the need — it's not a tier-1 fix but significantly stabilizes the position.
signings.notes: Guarantees TBD. Replaces/supplements Lavonte David's potential vacancy.

signings.analysis: Gainwell was the Steelers' MVP in 2025 — led the team with 73 receptions on 85 targets, totaling a career-high 1,023 scrimmage yards and 8 TDs. Two-year deal at $7M AAV is above RB market midpoint but justified given his versatility. He slots in as Bucky Irving's complement (change-of-pace, pass-catching back) essentially replacing Rachaad White on a better contract structure. Bucky Irving returns from injuries as the presumed lead back. RB room is solid but no standalone feature back — need partially addressed as depth is healthy but no workhorse upgrade.
signings.notes: Guarantees TBD. RFA tender also placed on Sean Tucker to retain him.

signings.analysis: One-year deal worth up to $6M with incentives. Muhammad had a career year in 2025 — 11 sacks, 9 TFLs, 20 QB hits with Detroit. That production at just $6M (max with incentives) is tremendous value IF it's repeatable. The question is whether his breakout in Year 8 was real or a product of playing alongside Aidan Hutchinson. One-year structure tells the whole story — this is a prove-it bridge deal, not a long-term investment. EDGE remains a real need heading into the draft. Yaya Diaby and David Walker (rehabbing torn ACL) are the other options. Need only partially addressed.
signings.notes: Max value $6M with incentives per ESPN. Reunites with former Lions teammate Alex Anzalone.

signings.analysis: One-year deal at $10M fills the void left by Logan Hall. Robinson (6'3", 320 lbs) is a physical run-stuffer who has played in both 3-4 and 4-3 systems. He lines up at 3-tech and 5-tech, mirroring Hall's alignment. Todd Bowles prioritized 'bigger players' this offseason and Robinson fits that mandate. However, the one-year structure is a significant limitation — this is a 30-year-old coming off a Panthers release, not a cornerstone piece. IDL remains a draft need for the future. Partially addresses the Hall departure for 2026 only.
signings.notes: Released by Panthers before signing with TB. Panthers were paying him $8.5M — TB gave him a raise to $10M.

signings.analysis: One-year deal to back up Baker Mayfield. Browning went 4-3 as a starter in 2023 with a 70.4% completion rate and 7 TDs. Given Mayfield's injury history (nagging injuries in 2025), upgrading the backup QB was a priority. Competes with Connor Bazelak for QB2. No need impact — QB is not a Bucs need with Mayfield under contract.
signings.notes: Contract terms not disclosed per Pewter Report/official sources.

signings.analysis: One-year, $1.8M deal. Killebrew follows new Bucs special teams coordinator Danny Smith from Pittsburgh. Pro Bowl special teamer in 2023 and 2024 with 70 solo and 91 combined special teams tackles — third-most in the league since 2016. Fills the Christian Izien void on special teams. However, he's coming off a significant knee injury that ended his 2025 season after 5 games. Purely a special teams/depth piece — does not address the starting safety depth need.
signings.notes: His arrival directly correlates with Danny Smith's hire as ST coordinator and Christian Izien's departure.

signings.analysis: Veteran minimum re-sign. Feeney filled in as a starting guard in 2025 when Ben Bredeson and Cody Mauch went on IR. One-year deal with no guaranteed money per Spotrac. Provides interior OL depth and familiarity with the system. Does not address any starting-caliber IOL need; purely insurance.
signings.notes: Confirmed via Peter Schrager/ESPN and Spotrac ($1.255M deal).

signings.analysis: One-year re-sign with no guaranteed money per reports. Kieft suffered a broken leg in Week 3 of 2025 and missed the rest of the season. He's a blocking TE and special teams ace — 24 kick-coverage tackles in 4 seasons, never below 60% special teams snap rate. With Otton locked up as the starter, Kieft provides blocking depth and ST value at TE2/TE3. Will have to earn a roster spot in training camp.
signings.notes: Confirmed by GM Jason Licht on social media. Contract terms undisclosed.

signings.analysis: Right-of-first-refusal tender placed on Tucker at the original-round tender value of ~$3.5M. Retains Tucker as RB3 depth behind Bucky Irving and Kenneth Gainwell. Low-risk retention of a young back with upside. No material need impact — purely depth.
signings.notes: Original-round RFA tender confirmed by buccaneers.com.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Mike Evans' departure to the 49ers (3yr/$42.4M, up to $60.4M with incentives) is the single biggest roster hole created this offseason. Evans was the true X-receiver, the team's all-time leading scorer, and the red zone anchor. Emeka Egbuka (Year 2, coming off injury), Chris Godwin (health concerns), and Jalen McMillan are the current options — none of them are proven WR1s. Tampa has not replaced this player in any meaningful way. WR should now be a top-3 draft priority.

Jamel Dean signed with Pittsburgh (3yr/$36.75M, $12M guaranteed). He was PFF's 3rd-highest graded CB in 2025 and the most consistent starter the Bucs had. Zyon McCollum (restructured) is the likely CB1 candidate but has not shown he can anchor a starting role for a full season. Benjamin Morrison is a rookie. Pewter Report confirmed the team will 'turn their attention to CB in FA' with names like Martin Emerson (CLE) and Rasul Douglas (MIA) being monitored. High-tier need — should be addressed in both remaining FA and early draft rounds.

Al-Quadin Muhammad (1yr/$6M max) partially addresses the hole left by Haason Reddick's departure (2.5 sacks) and Reddick's failure to resign. Muhammad's 11 sacks in 2025 are impressive but came in Year 8 of his career playing alongside Aidan Hutchinson — this is a bridge signing on a prove-it deal. Yaya Diaby exists as a complementary rusher and David Walker (ACL rehab, 4th-round pick 2025) has upside. EDGE remains a high-priority draft need — the one-year Muhammad deal does not resolve the long-term problem. Drops from critical to high but doesn't go away.

Alex Anzalone (2yr/$17M) is a genuine starter with a 65.0+ PFF grade for 3 straight years and elite coverage skills — best LB signing of Tampa's offseason. However, Lavonte David's retirement decision is still pending. If David retires (which appears likely given tone of reporting), the Bucs have Anzalone as a true starter but need a second quality LB. SirVocea Dennis is developing but unproven. Need is partially addressed but not fully — tier stays the same given David uncertainty. If David returns, tier drops to 3.

Logan Hall departed (2yr/$13.75M to Houston), and A'Shawn Robinson was signed for 1yr/$10M as a direct replacement. Robinson is a capable veteran run-stuffer who fits alongside Vita Vea in Bowles' system, but he's 30 and on a one-year deal — this is a stopgap, not a solution. Calijah Kancey (recovered from pec injury) adds depth. The one-year Robinson bridge keeps this at tier 2 — team still needs a long-term IDL answer in the draft.

Cade Otton re-signed on a 3yr/$30M deal ($10M AAV). He has back-to-back 59-catch seasons and is one of the most complete TEs in the NFC. The multi-year structure signals a real commitment. Ko Kieft is back on a one-year deal for depth/blocking/ST. TE is now a resolved position — no longer a draft priority.

Kenneth Gainwell (2yr/$14M) replaces Rachaad White on a better contract with more pass-catching upside. Bucky Irving is the lead back returning from foot/shoulder injuries. Sean Tucker retained via RFA tender. The backfield is adequately stocked — RB is no longer a draft focus unless a value pick emerges in rounds 4-7.

Christian Izien's departure (to Detroit) was flagged as a notable loss given his versatility. Miles Killebrew was signed for special teams/depth. However, Antoine Winfield Jr. and Tykee Smith remain an excellent starting duo — one of the youngest, brightest safety pairs in the NFL. Depth is thin (Marcus Banks, Rashad Wisdom, J.J. Roberts) but the starters are locked in. Need stays at tier 3 for depth purposes only — not a premium draft target.

Dan Feeney re-signed on a vet-minimum 1yr/$1.255M deal. Luke Goedeke (restructured, tackle converted to guard) and Cody Mauch (returning from IR) are the presumed starters. The interior OL is a concern — Mauch's injury history, the Goedeke conversion, and Feeney's minimal ceiling mean IOL depth is shaky. Need stays at tier 3 — a mid-round guard/center in the draft would be prudent.

key_additions.headline: 5-time Lions team captain brings elite coverage skills and leadership to stabilize the LB corps
Re-signed starter with back-to-back 59-catch seasons locks in TE for 3 years
2025 Steelers MVP (73 catches, 1,023 scrimmage yards) replaces Rachaad White as Bucky Irving's complement
Career-year pass rusher (11 sacks in 2025) signed on prove-it deal to anchor edge rush
6'3", 320-lb run-stuffer bridges Logan Hall departure alongside Vita Vea for 2026
Experienced backup (4-3 as 2023 starter) provides veteran insurance behind Baker Mayfield
Two-time Pro Bowl special teamer follows new ST coordinator Danny Smith from Pittsburgh

key_losses.headline: Franchise legend and all-time Bucs leading scorer departs after 12 seasons, creating a WR1 void
PFF's 3rd-highest graded CB in 2025 (46.9 passer rating allowed) leaves a massive hole at CB1
Versatile 2022 2nd-round pick departs for Houston after career-high 16 starts and 39 tackles
Four-year Buc with 4,100+ career scrimmage yards exits as Gainwell steps in as replacement
Versatile DB/nickel hybrid described as 'invaluable' departs, thinning secondary depth
One-year experiment (2.5 sacks in 13 games due to injuries) ends as Reddick tests free agency again

notes: Lavonte David's retirement/return decision is still pending as of 2026-03-16 and will significantly affect the LB need tier. All contract figures sourced from Spotrac, OverTheCap, and credentialed reporters (Schefter/Fowler/Pelissero/Aaron Wilson). Cade Otton and Ko Kieft guarantee details not yet publicly reported. Dan Feeney contract value per Spotrac ($1.255M). Al-Quadin Muhammad base salary not confirmed — ESPN reported max value of $6M with incentives. Contract restructures on Luke Goedeke and Zyon McCollum created additional cap flexibility mid-week. Cap space discrepancy: OverTheCap reports $40.7M available; Spotrac reports $972K — likely reflects different accounting of restructure savings and pending roster charges.
